course_description:
The sciences provide us with a myriad of pictures of the world – a world of energy fields, evolving organisms, and self-interested, reflective people. But how should we understand these pictures? What do scientific theories actually commit us to as real, what are the limits of their applicability, and how should apparently competing scientific pictures fit together? In short: what do scientific theories really tell us about reality?

This course approaches these questions from a philosophical perspective. It examines how scientific theories represent the world, what it means for a theory to explain, and how philosophical analysis helps clarify the assumptions, scope, and limits of scientific claims. Students are introduced to central philosophical issues concerning realism and anti-realism, explanation and causation, levels of organisation, scientific kinds, and the relationship between scientific theories and the phenomena they purport to describe.

To ground these philosophical questions, the course draws on case studies from the special sciences, including evolutionary theory, molecular biology, and psychology, but no prior scientific background is assumed. Instead, the focus is on understanding how philosophers analyse scientific theories: whether science offers a unified picture of reality, what kind of entities theories posit, whether life can be fully captured by physical descriptions, what evolution explains, whether genes and species are real, and whether mental disorders are disorders of the brain.

The course is designed to be accessible and engaging for both philosophy and science students. It develops philosophical skills in analysis, argumentation, and conceptual clarification, showing how philosophy contributes substantively to understanding contemporary science, and it develops tools for critically reflecting on what scientific theories mean, what they do and do not explain, and how scientific models shape our picture of the wo

Throughout the course, including in the final examination, students are assessed on their capacity to engage philosophically with the course material through clear reasoning and well-structured argument, not on their knowledge of scientific facts or technical detail.

course_aims: The sciences tell us what the stuff of the world is and how that stuff fits together to produce the world we observe. But what scientific theories really tell us about the world, and whether the sciences provide a unified picture at all, is not entirely obvious. The aim of this course is to explore the nature of scientific representations of the world, and to foster a critical and informed understanding of what science is telling us, how it explains reality, and what really us the stuff of the world.

learning_outcomes.description: Compare different philosophical frameworks for understanding what and how science tells us about reality.
Evaluate how different scientific theories represent reality, and the assumptions about the world and scientific inquiry on which those representations depend.
Recognise the role played by scientific representations in scientific discovery and practice, as well as public engagement with science.
Engage in contemporary debates about the scientific picture of the world in a critical and informed way.

assessment_details.task_description: Students are assessed on their participation and contribution to class discussions and engagement with written tasks completed in tutorials in up to 8 out of 10 weeks. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Written responses to stimulus prompts to be submitted in hardcopy to the tutor at the end of each tutorial.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
As this is secure assessment, there is no extension possible. Students who have a legitimate reason for not making it along to at least 8 out of 10 tutorials can negotiate alternative arrangements with the course coordinator.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
As this is secure assessment, there is no late submission possible.

assessment_details.title: Essay
assessment_details.task_description: Essay on a topic from the first half of the course. A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes; however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Essay to be submitted via Turnitin.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.topic: Tutorial Tutorials will run from Weeks 2 to 6, and then from Weeks 8 to 12. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Introduction: many scientific pictures, one world? General introduction to the course and subject matter: the idea that the sciences offer multiple 'pictures' of the world, raising the question of what scientific theories actually say about reality and where their limits lie. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

How do theories tell us about the world? We compare philosophical approaches to how theories relate to reality: Do they describe reality? Or just organise data? Do they predict phenomena? Or should they also explain how the world works? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Is a unified scientific view of our world possible? We examine whether the sciences can, or should, yield a unified picture of the world, and how apparently competing scientific pictures can be compatible, complementary, or genuinely in tension. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

What is scientific explanation? We develop an interventionist, graph-based account of scientific explanation and use it to analyse what causal models represent, what assumptions they embed, and what limitations they face. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Levels of organisation and explanatory diversity across sciences We learn how explanations differ across domains and levels (mechanistic, historical, functional, computational) and how interlevel relations shape what different sciences can legitimately be taken to say. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Is life more than physics? We consider whether biological phenomena are fully captured by purely physical descriptions, applying what we have learned about levels of organisation and explanation to clarify what the biological picture adds. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Evolution, function, and fitness: what is the evolutionary picture? We analyse what evolutionary theory claims about living systems, how talk about biological function should be interpreted, and what kind of theoretical quantity 'fitness' is supposed to be. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Are species and genes real? If so, what are they? We develop a framework for thinking about scientific classification and kinds, then apply it to debates about the reality and nature of species and genes across biological contexts. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Scientific psychology and folk psychology We investigate whether scientific psychology vindicates, revises, replaces, or eliminates everyday explanations in terms of beliefs and desires, and what psychological theories thereby commit us to as real. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Are mental disorders brain disorders? We evaluate competing accounts of what mental disorders are and whether they are best understood as brain disorders, with attention to levels of explanation; when we use a diagnostic label, are we naming something that exists as a unified phenomenon, or are we imposing a classificatory scheme that serves certain explanatory and practical purposes. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Can biological evolution explain social development? We critically examine evolutionary explanations of human behaviour and social phenomena, focusing on when it is okay to apply evolutionary theory, what counts as good evidence, and the risks of overextending a theory beyond what it can represent. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Chemical reactions, bonds, and substances We examine how chemistry represents the world through concepts such as substances, bonds, and reaction mechanisms, and what these representations commit us to about chemical kinds, explanation, and the relationship between chemical practice and underlying physical theory. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

The limits of scientific representations The course concludes by drawing together the semester’s themes to articulate the limits of scientific 'pictures': how is it that what theories can genuinely be taken to say is constrained? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
